summary: Joe Reis criticizes the flood of companies 'AI-washing' their products at tech conferences, comparing them to 19th-century carnival barkers selling snake oil. He argues that most organizations will fail with AI because they lack data fundamentals, and urges buyers to take a sober approach rather than falling for hype.
key_points: Most 'AI companies' at conferences are opportunistic rebrands from web3/blockchain
AI investments rarely make it past the POC stage
Good data hygiene is a prerequisite for AI success, not something AI can replace
Distinguish between generative AI (LLMs) and classical ML for practical applications
money_quotes: Most 'AI companies' I've seen are the modern-day equivalent - opportunistic carnival barkers hawking AI solutions to organizations who often don't understand what they're buying.
AI is an accelerator of good existing data hygiene and practices, not a replacement for it.
Your investment into AI will rarely leave the POC stage, if it even gets there.
I stick to Warren Buffett's approach of 'praise by name, criticize by category.'
key_insight: The AI gold rush of 2023 is producing more snake oil salesmen than genuine innovation, and companies would be better served fixing their data foundations than chasing AI hype.
